title: Unicode text and formatting

publication.changeSummary: Language-specific source-linked draft lesson for Unicode text and formatting; independent technical, source, pedagogy, accessibility, and runnable-example review remains required. canonicalExplanation.summary: Python str values are immutable Unicode text; splitting, joining, formatting, normalization, case handling, and encoding each solve a distinct boundary problem.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: A report builds rows with join and f-strings, then encodes UTF-8 only at the file or network boundary. Your goal is to implement and test robust text normalization, parsing and formatting. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: for, while and loop control; pf data selection; Numbers, booleans, None and text; Lists, tuples and slicing. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Language-specific operational model
sections.body: Python str values are immutable Unicode text; splitting, joining, formatting, normalization, case handling, and encoding each solve a distinct boundary problem. This model is applied through the declared Python: Beginner to Intermediate behavior rather than a language-neutral slogan.

sections.heading: Boundary and failure model
sections.body: User-perceived graphemes can span code points, casefold differs from lower, normalization forms change comparison, split without an argument treats whitespace specially, and bytes require an encoding boundary. The learner must reject this documented misconception: One Python character is always one displayed glyph and calling lower makes arbitrary Unicode text safely comparable.

includes: Use string methods, f-strings, splitting and joining while treating text as Unicode.
Implement and test robust text normalization, parsing and formatting.
excludes: User-perceived graphemes can span code points, casefold differs from lower, normalization forms change comparison, split without an argument treats whitespace specially, and bytes require an encoding boundary.

outcomes: Implement and test robust text normalization, parsing and formatting.

examples.title: Unicode text and formatting: direct language case
examples.situation: A report builds rows with join and f-strings, then encodes UTF-8 only at the file or network boundary.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Observe the concrete Python: Beginner to Intermediate state or tool result: A report builds rows with join and f-strings, then encodes UTF-8 only at the file or network boundary.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the result with this correction: Treat str as Unicode code points, define normalization and case policy for the domain, and encode or decode explicitly only at byte boundaries.
examples.result: The example meets the atomic outcome because Treat str as Unicode code points, define normalization and case policy for the domain, and encode or decode explicitly only at byte boundaries.

examples.title: Unicode text and formatting: boundary and transfer case
examples.situation: A case-insensitive identifier comparison normalizes and casefolds according to its documented policy instead of assuming lower handles every script.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Name what changed from the direct case: A case-insensitive identifier comparison normalizes and casefolds according to its documented policy instead of assuming lower handles every script.

Step 2 — Keep the language boundary explicit: User-perceived graphemes can span code points, casefold differs from lower, normalization forms change comparison, split without an argument treats whitespace specially, and bytes require an encoding boundary.
Step 3 — Reject the tempting but incorrect rule: One Python character is always one displayed glyph and calling lower makes arbitrary Unicode text safely comparable.
examples.result: This case demonstrates the declared outcome: Implement and test robust text normalization, parsing and formatting.

misconceptions.mistakenBelief: One Python character is always one displayed glyph and calling lower makes arbitrary Unicode text safely comparable.
misconceptions.correction: Treat str as Unicode code points, define normalization and case policy for the domain, and encode or decode explicitly only at byte boundaries.
misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Predict both examples and identify the first observable contradiction in this belief: One Python character is always one displayed glyph and calling lower makes arbitrary Unicode text safely comparable.

analogy.example: a flexible storeroom with ordered shelves, fixed labels, unique membership, lookup indexes, and shared nested containers can introduce Unicode text and formatting, after the canonical Python: Beginner to Intermediate rule is stated first.
usefulFor: lists, tuples, dictionaries, sets, aliasing, and text processing
limitations: hashability, shallow copying, Unicode normalization, and object identity require Python's exact data model
